title: The radio you wind by hand
summary: During a power cut in a Manila flat, a brother and sister argue about a small hand-cranked radio and what 'enough' really means.

body.speaker: Mia
body.text: The power is out again. My phone is almost dead. Now we have nothing.

body.speaker: Mia
body.text: A radio? It is old. Where are the batteries?

body.speaker: Ben
body.text: No batteries. You turn this little handle. You wind it. Listen.

body.speaker: Mia
body.text: It works! How? There is no power in the whole street.

body.speaker: Ben
body.text: Your arm is the power. One minute of winding gives thirty minutes of radio. It also has a small light.

body.speaker: Mia
body.text: That is clever. But it only does two things. My phone does a thousand things.

body.speaker: Ben
body.text: Yes. And your phone is dead, and my radio is on. Today, two things that work beat a thousand things that do not.

body.speaker: Mia
body.text: Fine, today. But tomorrow the power comes back. Then your radio is just a toy.

body.speaker: Ben
body.text: Maybe. But I like that it never needs me to buy anything. No battery. No bill. When everything stops, it still works. There is a kind of calm in that.

body.speaker: Mia
body.text: You make it sound like a friend.

body.speaker: Ben
body.text: In a storm, it is. Last year, after the typhoon, we had no power for five days. This radio told us where the water was. The phones were all dead by the second day.

body.speaker: Mia
body.text: I did not know that. I was at school in the city.

body.speaker: Ben
body.text: I know. That is why I keep it in the drawer. Not for every day. For the day everything else stops.

grammarNotes.title: Zero conditional with present simple
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu điều kiện loại 0 nói về sự thật luôn đúng: cả hai mệnh đề đều dùng hiện tại đơn. Trong bài: 'You turn this little handle. You wind it.' và ý 'One minute of winding gives thirty minutes of radio.' Khi A thì luôn có B — không phải tương lai, mà là quy luật.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The zero conditional states a fact that is always true: both clauses use the present simple. From the dialogue: 'One minute of winding gives thirty minutes of radio.' When A happens, B always follows — not a future guess, but a rule.

translationVi:
Mia: Lại mất điện rồi. Điện thoại em sắp hết pin. Giờ mình chẳng có gì cả.
Ben: Không phải chẳng có gì. Anh có cái này.
Mia: Cái đài á? Nó cũ rồi. Pin đâu?
Ben: Không cần pin. Em quay cái tay quay nhỏ này. Em lên dây cót cho nó. Nghe này.
Mia: Nó kêu rồi! Sao vậy? Cả con phố mất điện mà.
Ben: Cánh tay em là nguồn điện đấy. Quay một phút nghe được ba mươi phút đài. Nó còn có một cái đèn nhỏ nữa.
Mia: Tài thật. Nhưng nó chỉ làm được hai việc. Điện thoại em làm được cả nghìn việc.
Ben: Ừ. Mà điện thoại em chết rồi, còn cái đài của anh đang chạy. Hôm nay, hai việc chạy được hơn hẳn nghìn việc không chạy.
Mia: Được, hôm nay thôi. Nhưng mai có điện lại. Lúc đó cái đài của anh chỉ là đồ chơi.
Ben: Có thể. Nhưng anh thích chuyện nó chẳng bao giờ bắt anh phải mua gì. Không pin. Không hóa đơn. Khi mọi thứ ngừng, nó vẫn chạy. Trong đó có một kiểu yên tâm.
Mia: Anh nói cứ như nó là một người bạn.
Ben: Trong bão thì đúng vậy. Năm ngoái, sau cơn bão, nhà mình mất điện năm ngày. Cái đài này báo cho mình biết chỗ nào có nước. Đến ngày thứ hai là mọi điện thoại đều chết.
Mia: Em không biết chuyện đó. Hồi đó em đang học ở thành phố.
Ben: Anh biết. Vì vậy anh để nó trong ngăn kéo. Không phải cho mỗi ngày. Mà cho cái ngày mọi thứ khác ngừng lại.

questions.prompt: Why does Mia say they have nothing?
options: The power is out and her phone is almost dead
questions.explanation: 'The power is out again. My phone is almost dead.'

questions.prompt: How does Ben's radio get power?
options: You wind a little handle by hand
questions.explanation: 'You turn this little handle. You wind it.'

questions.prompt: How much radio does one minute of winding give?
questions.explanation: 'One minute of winding gives thirty minutes of radio.'
questions.hint: His line about the arm being the power.

questions.prompt: What else does the radio have?
What does Ben say about 'two things' versus 'a thousand things'?
options: Today, two things that work beat a thousand that do not
A thousand things are always better
questions.explanation: 'Today, two things that work beat a thousand things that do not.'
questions.hint: His reply to Mia about the phone.

questions.prompt: Why does Ben like that the radio needs no battery and no bill?
options: When everything stops, it still works — there is calm in that
questions.explanation: 'When everything stops, it still works. There is a kind of calm in that.'

questions.prompt: What happened last year after the typhoon?
options: No power for five days; the radio told them where the water was
questions.explanation: 'after the typhoon, we had no power for five days. This radio told us where the water was.'

questions.prompt: When did the phones die during the typhoon?
questions.explanation: 'The phones were all dead by the second day.'

questions.prompt: Why does Ben keep the radio in the drawer?
options: Not for every day, but for the day everything else stops
questions.explanation: 'Not for every day. For the day everything else stops.'

options: A simple, self-powered tool can matter most exactly when the clever ones fail.
Old radios are better than phones for everything.
You should never use a phone.
questions.explanation: The radio's worth shows in the power cut and the typhoon.

Mia: Fine, today. But tomorrow the power comes back. Then your radio is just a {{b7}}.

Ben: In a storm, it is. Last year, after the {{b9}}, we had no power for five days. This radio told us where the water was. The phones were all dead by the second day.

blanks.explanation: I keep it in the drawer.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Ben keeps a simple tool for 'the day everything else stops'. What is one thing in your life that you depend on completely? Is there a simpler backup you could keep for the day it fails?
